cold_watch.immediate_felt_reaction: The ad feels like a playable mobile game loop: one tiny avatar makes a huge frozen map become manageable.
cold_watch.moment_of_curiosity: Curiosity peaks when the ad reveals survivors queued outside the gate after the player has started building the base.
cold_watch.moment_of_confusion_or_boredom: The middle stretch repeats clearing, spending, and upgrading without a new verbal promise, so non-game viewers may lose the thread.
cold_watch.most_memorable_image: Long survivor lines waiting outside a small warm base carved into an enormous snowy forest.

evidence_timeline.observation: A tiny parka-wearing avatar starts in a small fenced clearing with a lit campfire, a green resource counter at 0, and dense snowy forest filling almost the entire vertical frame.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The viewer immediately understands scale, scarcity, and a survival problem that can be acted on with one controllable character.

evidence_timeline.observation: The avatar carves a narrow path through the snow-packed tree wall and then harvests a bright green tree patch beside a build tile.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The map changes visibly after each action, making progress feel tactile and easy to understand without voiceover.
evidence_timeline.mechanism: manual action creates visible terrain change

evidence_timeline.observation: The camera returns to the campfire hub, where a building icon with a 120 requirement appears while the avatar continues clearing snowy trees nearby.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The viewer sees a simple resource gate: clear space, gather materials, then unlock the next structure.

evidence_timeline.observation: A large queue of yellow-jacket survivors waits outside the base gate while the avatar applies green resource bundles to the entrance tile.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The base is no longer just decoration; there is external pressure from people waiting to be rescued or admitted.
evidence_timeline.mechanism: queued demand turns building into rescue pressure

evidence_timeline.observation: Inside the base, snowy land has become bright green forest, workers move through the clearing, and green cash bundles flow into the gate tile while survivors wait outside.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The creative rewards attention with a clear before-after state: the frozen camp is becoming alive and productive.

evidence_timeline.observation: A blue-roofed hut and sawmill-like machine appear, with the avatar spending green currency and carrying a long stack of wood to a 100-cost tile.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The ad layers a production loop on top of the rescue loop, suggesting depth without explaining menus or strategy systems.

evidence_timeline.observation: The base now contains campfire, hut, sawmill, stacked lumber, green forest patches, survivor lanes, and a 200 resource counter while the avatar spends on another tile.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The player fantasy shifts from surviving alone to managing a growing settlement with multiple simultaneous needs.
evidence_timeline.mechanism: single-worker loop expands into system management

evidence_timeline.observation: A zoomed-out view shows a multi-room base carved out of the snow forest, long survivor queues, and multiple 300-cost build tiles still waiting.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The unfinished build pads create completion tension: the viewer can imagine the next upgrades before the ad ends.

evidence_timeline.observation: The end card overlays stacked gameplay screenshots, Whiteout Survival branding, a large partially readable headline, and a partially readable NO.1 winter-theme claim.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The ad shifts from simulated play to app-store style proof, but the headline legibility is weaker than the gameplay demonstration.

core_mechanism.name: overwhelm-to-order rescue loop
core_mechanism.why_it_catches_attention: It opens with extreme visual imbalance: one tiny controllable worker against an oversized frozen wilderness.
core_mechanism.why_it_might_persuade: The ad repeatedly shows small actions producing visible world changes, new buildings, queues, resources, and unfinished upgrade prompts.
core_mechanism.what_cannot_be_copied: Do not copy Whiteout Survival branding, winter art direction, specific UI, gameplay screenshots, music, captions, claims, or end-card composition.

creative_intent.creative_label: Whiteout Survival Rescue Base Loop Ad
creative_intent.creative_type: Playable-style mobile game demo / progression fantasy / direct response
creative_intent.likely_hypothesis: If cold mobile game viewers see one worker convert a hostile frozen map into a functioning rescue base, they will understand the core loop and feel pulled to install.
creative_intent.target_audience: Casual mobile players who like survival, idle resource loops, base building, and visible map-cleanup progression.
creative_intent.awareness_stage: Genre-aware to product-aware mobile game viewers.
creative_intent.likely_kpi: Click-through rate, store visits, cost per install, and early gameplay engagement.
creative_intent.offer_strategy: Replace a discount or feature list with a satisfying playable-looking loop and a branded end card.
creative_intent.marketer_job_to_be_done: Make the game instantly legible as a rescue-and-build loop before the viewer scrolls away.
unknowns: No media-buy, store-page, install, retention, or ROAS data is available.
The video does not prove whether the shown gameplay matches the first playable session.
End-card text is partially unreadable in the sampled frame.

distilled_core.core_thesis: The creative sells the game as a rescue-and-build loop where one tiny action turns frozen chaos into visible order.
distilled_core.mechanism_formula: isolation hook -> tactile clearing -> rescue pressure -> base upgrades -> branded install close
distilled_core.belief_shift_summary: The viewer moves from seeing an overwhelming frozen wilderness to believing the game offers immediate control, rescue stakes, and steady settlement growth.
distilled_core.why_it_works: It exploits completion pressure, visible before-after progress, and the pleasure of turning a hostile map into a productive base.
distilled_core.where_it_breaks: The midsection can feel repetitive, and the final proof card is less legible than the gameplay loop.
distilled_core.submagic_transfer: For Submagic, translate the mechanism into a raw-to-publishable workflow: show one creator action triggering visible captions, cuts, zooms, and output growth.
steal: Open with an overwhelming state that one action can fix.
Show visible state change after every beat.
Use queued demand to create urgency.
avoid: Do not copy the winter map, UI, brand, or game screenshots.
Do not use unverifiable ranking claims.
Do not let the close card become less readable than the demo.
test_next: Test a shorter version that reaches survivor pressure before 15 seconds.
Test a clearer end card with one readable install reason.
Test a Submagic workflow demo built around queued creator clips.

core_understanding.human_truth: People enjoy feeling that a chaotic system can be made safe, productive, and complete through a series of small obvious actions.

belief_shift.before: This is an overwhelming frozen world with scarce resources and too many unknown needs.
belief_shift.after: This is a legible loop: clear, gather, unlock, rescue, build, and repeat toward a bigger base.
belief_shift.bridge: Each visual beat turns a small action into a visible map change, resource spend, or new structure.

tension_stack: The avatar is tiny compared with the huge snowy forest.
The resource counter begins at 0, making scarcity explicit.
Build tiles show numeric costs that are not yet affordable.
Survivors queue outside the gate, giving the base-building a human deadline.
Unfinished pads remain visible near the end, keeping completion pressure open.

proof_ladder.claim: The world starts hostile and resource-scarce.
proof_ladder.proof_type: first-frame scale contrast and zero resource UI
proof_ladder.weakness: The opening has no explicit caption explaining the goal.

proof_ladder.claim: Player actions visibly change the map.
proof_ladder.weakness: The ad does not show whether these actions are actual player controls or staged creative.

proof_ladder.claim: The base has external rescue pressure.
proof_ladder.proof_type: survivor queue and gate resource payment
proof_ladder.weakness: The survivors' exact role is inferred visually, not stated in text.

proof_ladder.claim: The loop escalates into base management.
proof_ladder.proof_type: hut, sawmill, lumber stacks, multiple build pads, and resource counters
proof_ladder.weakness: The ad shows many objects but no clear feature explanation.

proof_ladder.claim: The brand and install prompt arrive after the gameplay proof.
proof_ladder.proof_type: stacked screenshot end card and Whiteout Survival branding
proof_ladder.weakness: The sampled end-card text is partly unreadable.

viewer_state_timeline.trigger: Tiny avatar, warm campfire, resource counter at 0, and overwhelming snow forest.
Path clearing and harvesting create visible progress.
Survivors queue outside the gate while the avatar spends resources to unlock access.
Hut, sawmill, lumber stacks, and resource payments make the player look increasingly capable.
Zoomed-out base and end card show unfinished pads, screenshots, and brand close.

core_understanding.skeptic_read: A skeptical viewer may read this as polished ad-only gameplay because the loop is highly simplified, UI costs jump quickly, and the end-card ranking claim is not independently substantiated in the video.
core_understanding.transfer_principle_for_submagic: Do not borrow the game surface; borrow the psychological loop of overwhelming starting state, one obvious action, visible transformation, queued demand, and an unfinished next step.
counterfactuals: If survivor pressure appeared earlier, the creative would likely become more emotionally legible before the first retention drop.
If the ad opened with only the end card, it would lose the tactile proof that makes the loop understandable.
If every resource action had a clearer before-after label, non-game viewers might understand the stakes faster.

scores.improvement_note: The first frame is visually strong, but a clearer goal cue could broaden comprehension.
The clear-gather-build loop is readable from visuals alone.
Survivor queues add stakes, but they arrive after several purely mechanical beats.
The base visibly grows from campfire to multi-structure settlement.
The 56-second runtime gives proof depth but repeats harvesting and spending beats.
The gameplay screenshots are clear, but headline and ranking copy are partly unreadable in the sampled end frame.
The state-change mechanism transfers well; the game surface and brand assets do not.
Any adaptation must avoid the Whiteout Survival brand, winter map, UI, screenshots, and ranking claim.

tiktok.notes: Strong vertical visual hook and satisfying loop; survivor stakes should arrive sooner for short attention spans.

reels.notes: The visual transformation fits Reels, but small UI and late end-card text may be harder to read on quick scroll.

shorts.notes: The loop is understandable, but the runtime is long for a purely mechanical demo without spoken structure.

meta_paid.notes: Playable-style progression and install-oriented end card fit paid mobile-game acquisition creative.

linkedin.notes: The creative is consumer mobile-game acquisition and has no business or professional framing.

drop_off_hypotheses: Non-game viewers may drop during the first 8 seconds if they do not infer the goal from the campfire, resource counter, and clearing action.
The 14-34 second stretch may lose attention because it repeats clearing, spending, and waiting-line beats before a major new payoff.
The final CTA may underperform relative to the gameplay proof because the sampled end-card copy is less legible than the gameplay visuals.
risks_and_unknowns: No real performance metrics are provided, so all scores are creative-signal judgments only.
The video does not establish whether the shown loop is representative of actual first-session gameplay.
The OCR is mostly noisy, so visual evidence carries most claims.
The partially readable NO.1 winter-theme claim should not be reused without independent substantiation.
Submagic adaptation should borrow the transformation mechanism, not the competitor's script, visuals, captions, brand identity, gameplay, music, or proprietary claims.
